formulas.name: Decomposition Process
formulas.formula: Organic matter → Simple compounds → Inorganic compounds → Release of nutrients
formulas.variables: Organic matter, Simple compounds, Inorganic compounds, Nutrients
formulas.notes: Decomposition is the process by which organic matter is broken down into simpler compounds and released into the environment.

formulas.name: Energy Flow in an Ecosystem
formulas.formula: Primary producers → Primary consumers → Secondary consumers → Decomposers
formulas.variables: Primary producers, Primary consumers, Secondary consumers, Decomposers
formulas.notes: Energy flow in an ecosystem starts with primary producers and moves through primary consumers, secondary consumers, and finally to decomposers.

formulas.name: Energy Pyramid/Ladder
formulas.formula: Base of pyramid (Producers) → Narrowest width (Primary consumers) → Widest width (Decomposers)
formulas.notes: An energy pyramid represents the energy flow in an ecosystem with producers at the base and decomposers at the top.

formulas.name: Biodiversity
formulas.formula: Biodiversity = Genetic diversity + Species diversity + Ecosystem diversity
formulas.variables: Genetic diversity, Species diversity, Ecosystem diversity
formulas.notes: Biodiversity is the total variety of life on Earth, comprising genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity.

formulas.name: Succession
formulas.notes: Succession is the process of change in the composition of an ecosystem over time, occurring after primary or secondary disturbances.

formulas.name: Trophic Level
formulas.formula: Trophic level = Position in the food chain
formulas.variables: Trophic level, Position in the food chain
formulas.notes: Trophic level refers to the position of an organism in the food chain, with primary producers at level 1 and top carnivores at level 5.

formulas.name: Cycles
formulas.formula: Nutrient cycles → Water cycle → Carbon cycle → Oxygen cycle
formulas.variables: Nutrient cycles, Water cycle, Carbon cycle, Oxygen cycle
formulas.notes: Cycles refer to the continuous movement of nutrients and materials through an ecosystem, including nutrient cycles, water cycle, carbon cycle, and oxygen cycle.
